1→# Agent Guidelines for Formal Conjectures
     2→
     3→This document provides guidelines for AI agents working on the Formal Conjectures repository.
     4→
     5→## Repository Structure
     6→
     7→### Main Directories
     8→
     9→- **`FormalConjectures/`**: Contains formalisations of conjectures, organized by source:
    10→  - `ErdosProblems/` - Problems from [erdosproblems.com](https://www.erdosproblems.com/)
    11→  - `Paper/` - Problems from research papers
    12→  - `Arxiv/` - Problems from arXiv papers (organized by arXiv ID)
    13→  - `Books/` - Problems from mathematics books
    14→  - `Wikipedia/` - Problems from Wikipedia
    15→  - `OptimizationConstants/` - Problems from [Tao's Optimization Constants](https://teorth.github.io/optimizationproblems/)
    16→  - Other sources as appropriate
    17→
    18→- **`FormalConjectures/Util/`**: Repository infrastructure and utilities:
    19→  - `Attributes/` - Defines the `category` and `AMS` attributes
    20→  - `Answer.lean` - Implements the `answer()` elaborator for problems requiring answers
    21→  - `Linters/` - Custom linters for the repository
    22→  - `ProblemImports.lean` - Standard imports for problem files
    23→
    24→- **`FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`**: Definitions, lemmas, and basic API suitable for upstreaming to Mathlib:
    25→  - **IMPORTANT**: No `sorry` is allowed in this directory
    26→  - Follows Mathlib's directory structure (e.g., `Topology/`, `Algebra/`, `NumberTheory/`)
    27→  - Contains supporting definitions and lemmas needed for problem statements
    28→  - Code here should be of Mathlib quality and eventually PR-able to Mathlib
    29→
    30→## Formalisation Conventions
    31→
    32→### Namespaces
    33→
    34→Each problem file should define its content in a dedicated namespace:
    35→
    36→```lean
    37→namespace Erdos10
    38→
    39→-- definitions, theorems, variants here
    40→
    41→end Erdos10
    42→```
    43→
    44→For variants of a problem, use dotted notation within the same namespace:
    45→```lean
    46→theorem main_conjecture : ... := by
    47→  sorry
    48→
    49→theorem main_conjecture.variants.special_case : ... := by
    50→  sorry
    51→```
    52→
    53→### The `category` Attribute
    54→
    55→Every theorem/lemma should have exactly one `category` attribute indicating its type:
    56→
    57→**Values:**
    58→- `@[category high_school]` - High school level math problem
    59→- `@[category undergraduate]` - Undergraduate level math problem
    60→- `@[category graduate]` - Graduate level math problem
    61→- `@[category research open]` - Open research problem (no accepted solution exists)
    62→- `@[category research solved]` - Solved research problem (informal proof widely accepted)
    63→- `@[category research formally solved using <kind> at "link"]` - Formally solved problem
    64→  - `<kind>` must be one of: `formal_conjectures`, `lean4`, or `other_system`
    65→- `@[category test]` - Sanity check or unit test for definitions
    66→- `@[category API]` - Basic theory around a new definition
    67→
    68→**Examples:**
    69→```lean
    70→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    71→theorem riemann_hypothesis : ... := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→@[category test, AMS 11]
    75→theorem sanity_check : ¬ SomeBadProperty := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→@[category API]
    79→lemma basic_property_of_new_definition : ... := by exact ...
    80→```
    81→
    82→### The `AMS` Attribute
    83→
    84→Every problem should have at least one AMS subject classification number from the [AMS MSC2020](https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/msc/pdfs/classifications2020.pdf).
    85→
    86→**Common subjects:**
    87→- `3` - Mathematical logic and foundations
    88→- `5` - Combinatorics
    89→- `11` - Number theory
    90→- `14` - Algebraic geometry
    91→- `54` - General topology
    92→- `60` - Probability theory
    93→
    94→Multiple subjects can be specified:
    95→```lean
    96→@[AMS 5 11]  -- Both combinatorics and number theory
    97→theorem erdos_problem : ... := by
    98→  sorry
    99→```
   100→
   101→**In VS Code:** Use `#AMS` command to list all available subjects and their numbers. Hover over a number to see its description.
   102→
   103→Full list of AMS subjects
   104→| Code | Name | Keywords |
   105→|------|------|----------|
   106→| 00 | General and overarching topics | general |
   107→| 03 | Mathematical logic and foundations | logic, set theory, model theory |
   108→| 05 | Combinatorics | combinatorics, graph theory, enumeration |
   109→| 06 | Order | order, lattice, poset, partial order |
   110→| 08 | General algebraic systems | universal algebra, algebraic system |
   111→| 11 | Number theory | number theory, prime, arithmetic, diophantine |
   112→| 12 | Field theory and polynomials | field, polynomial, galois |
   113→| 13 | Commutative algebra | commutative, ring, ideal, module |
   114→| 14 | Algebraic geometry | algebraic geometry, variety, scheme |
   115→| 15 | Linear algebra | linear algebra, matrix, vector |
   116→| 16 | Associative rings and algebras | associative, ring, algebra |
   117→| 17 | Nonassociative rings and algebras | lie algebra, jordan |
   118→| 18 | Category theory; homological algebra | category, functor, homological |
   119→| 19 | K-theory | k-theory |
   120→| 20 | Group theory and generalizations | group, finite group, representation |
   121→| 22 | Topological groups + Lie groups | topological group, lie group |
   122→| 26 | Real functions | real analysis, real function, derivative |
   123→| 28 | Measure and integration | measure, integration, lebesgue |
   124→| 30 | Functions of a complex variable | complex analysis, holomorphic, analytic |
   125→| 31 | Potential theory | potential, harmonic function, subharmonic |
   126→| 32 | Complex analysis | several complex variables, complex manifold |
   127→| 33 | Special functions | special function, gamma, zeta, bessel |
   128→| 34 | Ordinary differential equations | ode, differential equation |
   129→| 35 | Partial differential equations | pde, partial differential |
   130→| 37 | Dynamical systems and ergodic theory | dynamical, ergodic, chaos |
   131→| 39 | Difference and functional equations | difference equation, functional equation |
   132→| 40 | Sequences + series + summability | sequence, series, convergence |
   133→| 41 | Approximations and expansions | approximation, expansion, interpolation |
   134→| 42 | Harmonic analysis on Euclidean spaces | harmonic analysis, fourier |
   135→| 43 | Abstract harmonic analysis | abstract harmonic |
   136→| 44 | Integral transforms + operational calculus | integral transform, laplace |
   137→| 45 | Integral equations | integral equation |
   138→| 46 | Functional analysis | functional analysis, banach, hilbert |
   139→| 47 | Operator theory | operator, spectral |
   140→| 49 | Calc. of vars. and opt. ctrl.; optimiz. | calculus of variations, optimal control |
   141→| 51 | Geometry | geometry, euclidean, projective |
   142→| 52 | Convex and discrete geometry | convex, polytope, discrete geometry |
   143→| 53 | Differential geometry | differential geometry, manifold, riemannian |
   144→| 54 | General topology | topology, topological space, compact |
   145→| 55 | Algebraic topology | algebraic topology, homotopy, homology |
   146→| 57 | Manifolds and cell complexes | manifold, cell complex |
   147→| 58 | Global analysis + analysis on manifolds | global analysis |
   148→| 60 | Probability theory | probability, stochastic, random |
   149→| 62 | Statistics | statistics, estimation, hypothesis |
   150→| 65 | Numerical analysis | numerical, approximation, algorithm |
   151→| 68 | Computer science | computer science, complexity, algorithm |
   152→| 70 | Mechanics of particles and systems | mechanics, particle, celestial |
   153→| 74 | Mechanics of deformable solids | elasticity, solid mechanics |
   154→| 76 | Fluid mechanics | fluid, navier-stokes |
   155→| 78 | Optics + electromagnetic theory | optics, electromagnetic |
   156→| 80 | Classical thermodynamics + heat transfer | thermodynamics, heat |
   157→| 81 | Quantum theory | quantum, quantum mechanics |
   158→| 82 | Statistical mechanics | statistical mechanics |
   159→| 83 | Relativity and gravitational theory | relativity, gravity, einstein |
   160→| 85 | Astronomy and astrophysics | astronomy, astrophysics |
   161→| 86 | Geophysics | geophysics |
   162→| 90 | Mathematical programming | optimization, linear programming |
   163→| 91 | Game theory | game theory, economics |
   164→| 92 | Biology and other natural sciences | biology, mathematical biology |
   165→| 93 | Systems theory; control | control theory, systems theory |
   166→| 94 | Information and communication + circuits | information theory, coding theory |
   167→| 97 | Mathematics education | education |
   168→
   169→### The `answer()` Elaborator
   170→
   171→For problems that ask questions requiring specific answers (not just yes/no), use `answer(sorry)`:
   172→
   173→```lean
   174→/-- Does $P$ hold? -/
   175→@[category research open]
   176→theorem problem_name : answer(sorry) ↔ SomeProperty := by
   177→  sorry
   178→```
   179→
   180→Note that if the statement of the theorem depends on some variables, then the quantification should
   181→happen *after* the `answer(sorry)`, i.e.
   182→```lean
   183→/-- Does $P(n)$ hold for all $n$? -/
   184→@[category research open]
   185→theorem problem_name : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n, P n := by
   186→  sorry
   187→```
   188→rather than
   189→```lean
   190→/-- Does $P(n)$ hold for all $n$? -/
   191→@[category research open]
   192→theorem problem_name (n) : answer(sorry) ↔ P n := by
   193→  sorry
   194→```
   195→
   196→
   197→When the problem is solved:
   198→- Replace `answer(sorry)` with `answer(True)` or `answer(False)` as appropriate
   199→- Update the category to `research solved` or `research formally solved ...`
   200→
   201→**Note:** Providing a term inside `answer()` does NOT automatically mean the problem is mathematically solved - trivial or tautological answers don't count as solutions.
   202→
   203→### File Organization
   204→
   205→1. **One problem per file** (with flexibility for closely related variants)
   206→2. **Copyright header required** (see template in README.md, use year 2026)
   207→3. **Module docstring** with references:
   208→   ```lean
   209→   /-!
   210→   # Problem Name
   211→
   212→   *Reference:* [Title](URL)
   213→
   214→   Brief description if needed.
   215→   -/
   216→   ```
   217→4. **Import structure**:
   218→   - Problem files: Import `FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`
   219→   - ForMathlib files: Import only necessary Mathlib modules
   220→5. If a problem fits in several directories then it should stated in one directory rather than copied
   221→   accross several. In other directories, one can simply add a file with a declaration pointing
   222→   to the original, e.g.
   223→   ```lean
   224→   @[category research open, AMS 11]
   225→   theorem pointer_to_original : type_of% my_original_theorem := by
   226→     sorry
   227→   ```
   228→   See for example `FormalConjectures/GreensOpenProblems/81.lean`.
   229→
   230→### Variants and Related Results
   231→
   232→Variants should be in the same file as the main conjecture:
   233→
   234→```lean
   235→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   236→theorem main_conjecture : MainStatement := by
   237→  sorry
   238→
   239→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   240→theorem main_conjecture.variants.weaker_version : WeakerStatement := by
   241→  sorry
   242→
   243→@[category test, AMS 11]
   244→theorem main_conjecture.variants.small_cases : MainStatement with (n < 100) := by
   245→  interval_cases n <;> decide
   246→```
   247→
   248→## Lean and Mathlib Style Guidelines
   249→
   250→### Naming Conventions
   251→
   252→Follow [Mathlib's naming conventions](https://leanprover-community.github.io/contribute/naming.html). Unlike Lean 3, Lean 4 uses a combination of `snake_case`, `lowerCamelCase`, and `UpperCamelCase`:
   253→
   254→#### Capitalization Rules
   255→
   256→1. **Terms of Props** (proofs, theorem names) use `snake_case`:
   257→   ```lean
   258→   theorem fermat_last_theorem : FermatLastTheorem := by sorry
   259→   lemma add_comm (a b : ℕ) : a + b = b + a := by sorry
   260→   ```
   261→
   262→2. **Props and Types** (inductive types, structures, classes) use `UpperCamelCase`:
   263→   ```lean
   264→   class HasGδSingletons (X : Type*) [TopologicalSpace X] : Prop
   265→   structure MyStructure where
   266→     field1 : ℕ
   267→     field2 : String
   268→   inductive Color where
   269→     | Red | Green | Blue
   270→   ```
   271→
   272→3. **Functions** are named the same way as their return values:
   273→   - A function `A → B → C` is named as though it's a term of type `C`
   274→   - If `C` is a `Prop`, use `snake_case`
   275→   - If `C` is a `Type`, use `lowerCamelCase`
   276→
   277→4. **All other terms of Types** use `lowerCamelCase`:
   278→   ```lean
   279→   def leftFactorial (n : ℕ) : ℕ := ∑ i in Finset.range n, i!
   280→   def myFunction (x : ℕ) : ℕ := x + 1
   281→   ```
   282→
   283→5. **UpperCamelCase in snake_case contexts**: When something named with `UpperCamelCase` is part of something named with `snake_case`, it is referenced in `lowerCamelCase`:
   284→   ```lean
   285→   theorem fermat_last_theorem : FermatLastTheorem := by sorry
   286→   theorem nat_factorial_pos (n : ℕ) : 0 < n! := by sorry
   287→   ```
   288→
   289→6. **Acronyms**: Written as a group in upper-/lowercase depending on the first character:
   290→   - `LE`, `LT`, `GE`, `GT` (when in `UpperCamelCase` context)
   291→   - `le`, `lt`, `ge`, `gt` (when in `lowerCamelCase` context)
   292→
   293→7. **Structure fields and constructors**: Follow the same rules (1-6) as top-level declarations
   294→
   295→#### Common Naming Patterns
   296→
   297→- `add_comm`, `mul_assoc` - operation + property
   298→- `Nat.factorial_pos` - namespace + definition + property
   299→- `foo_of_bar` - derive `foo` from hypothesis `bar`
   300→- `bar_iff_foo` - equivalence between `bar` and `foo`
   301→- `foo_le_bar` - inequality statement
   302→
   303→#### Exceptions
   304→
   305→Some rare exceptions exist for consistency:
   306→- `Ne` (not `NE`) follows `Eq`
   307→- Intervals: `Set.Icc`, `Set.Iic` (capital `I` despite convention)
   308→- Some legacy structure fields may be lowercase
   309→
   310→**Note**: In this repository, most declarations are theorems (terms of Props), so you'll primarily use `snake_case`.
   311→
   312→### Code Quality
   313→
   314→- **Use Unicode math symbols** where appropriate: `∀`, `∃`, `∈`, `⊆`, `∧`, `∨`, `¬`, etc.
   315→- **Format code properly**: Use consistent indentation (2 spaces)
   316→- **Add docstrings** with the math written in Latex for definitions and main theorems:
   317→  ```lean
   318→  /--
   319→  The left factorial of $n$, defined as $0! + 1! + 2! + ... + (n-1)!$
   320→  -/
   321→  def left_factorial (n : ℕ) := ...
   322→  ```
   323→- **Use `local notation`** for problem-specific notation within namespaces
   324→- **Avoid unnecessary type annotations** when Lean can infer them
   325→- **Use `by` tactic mode** for sorries: `by sorry` (not just `sorry`)
   326→- **Do not try to add a suggested informal proof for an open research problem**
   327→- **Avoid using tactics like `native_decide` in proofs**. These are completely banned in `FormalConjecturesForMathlib`
   328→  and may be considered on a case by case basis for in `category test` statements in problem files
   329→  when the goal is a large computation.
   330→
   331→### Imports
   332→
   333→- Be specific with imports - don't import more than needed
   334→- In FormalConjecturesForMathlib, import only from Mathlib
   335→- In problem files, import only `FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`, unless you are adding a
   336→   pointer to another problem or need to state an implication.
   337→
   338→## Agent-Specific Requirements
   339→
   340→### Before Submitting
   341→
   342→**CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS:**
   343→
   344→1. **`lake build` MUST pass** without errors before submitting for review
   345→   - Run `lake build` in the repository root
   346→   - Fix all compilation errors
   347→   - Ensure all dependencies are properly imported
   348→
   349→2. **`sorry` usage restrictions**:
   350→   - ✅ **ALLOWED**: In `FormalConjectures/` for benchmark problem statements
   351→     ```lean
   352→     @[category research open]
   353→     theorem open_conjecture : Statement := by sorry
   354→     ```
   355→   - ❌ **NOT ALLOWED**: In `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`
   356→     ```lean
   357→     -- WRONG - will be rejected
   358→     def helper_function : Type := sorry
   359→     lemma helper_lemma : P := by sorry
   360→     ```
   361→   - Exception: `answer(sorry)` is allowed as a placeholder for unknown answers
   362→
   363→3. **Completeness**:
   364→   - No placeholder definitions (e.g., `def foo : Type := sorry`)
   365→   - No incomplete type annotations or holes
   366→   - All referenced definitions must exist
   367→   - All imports must be correct
   368→
   369→4. **Clean code**:
   370→   - Remove commented-out code
   371→   - Remove debug statements
   372→   - No unused imports
   373→   - No unnecessary intermediate definitions
   374→
   375→### Quality Checklist
   376→
   377→Before considering your work complete, verify:
   378→
   379→- [ ] File has correct copyright header (current year)
   380→- [ ] Module docstring with reference links present
   381→- [ ] Namespace properly opened and closed
   382→- [ ] All theorems have `category` attribute
   383→- [ ] All theorems have at least one `AMS` subject
   384→- [ ] Names follow snake_case convention
   385→- [ ] `lake build` succeeds
   386→- [ ] No `sorry` in FormalConjecturesForMathlib/
   387→- [ ] Docstrings present for main definitions
   388→- [ ] Code properly formatted and readable
   389→
   390→### Testing Definitions
   391→
   392→When adding new definitions to FormalConjecturesForMathlib/, include basic sanity checks:
   393→
   394→```lean
   395→-- In FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Topology/MyDefinition.lean
   396→class MyNewClass (X : Type*) : Prop where
   397→  property : SomeProperty X
   398→
   399→-- In FormalConjectures/Paper/ProblemUsingMyDefinition.lean
   400→@[category test]
   401→theorem myNewClass_sanity_check :
   402→    MyNewClass SomeConcreteType := by
   403→  constructor
   404→  exact proof_of_property
   405→```
   406→
   407→### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
   408→
   409→❌ **DON'T:**
   410→- Use `sorry` outside of problem statement proofs
   411→- Create files without copyright headers
   412→- Forget to add `category` and `AMS` attributes
   413→- Submit code that doesn't compile
   414→- Add large proofs (this is a benchmark repository, not a proof repository)
   415→- Use camelCase for theorem names
   416→- Create placeholder definitions in FormalConjecturesForMathlib/
   417→
   418→✅ **DO:**
   419→- Follow existing file patterns in the repository
   420→- Keep formalisations clean and minimal
   421→- Add references to sources. These should all appear in the module docstring reference list, and
   422→  ideally each conjecture statement docstring should have a reference.
   423→- Use namespaces appropriately
   424→- Test that `lake build` works
   425→- Add variants in the same file as the main problem
   426→- Include basic API for new definitions
   427→
   428→## Getting Help
   429→
   430→- Check existing files for examples: `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/10.lean`, `FormalConjectures/Paper/Kurepa.lean`
   431→- Use `#AMS` command in VS Code to see available subject classifications
   432→- Refer to [Mathlib naming conventions](https://leanprover-community.github.io/contribute/naming.html)
   433→- See [main README.md](./README.md) for contribution workflow
   434→- See [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md) for CLA requirements
   435→
   436→## Example Template
   437→
   438→```lean
   439→/-
   440→Copyright YYYY The Formal Conjectures Authors.
   441→    (Replace YYYY with the current year, e.g., 2026 for work done in 2026)
   442→
   443→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
   444→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
   445→You may obtain a copy of the License at
   446→
   447→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
   448→
   449→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
   450→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
   451→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
   452→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   453→limitations under the License.
   454→-/
   455→
   456→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
   457→
   458→/-!
   459→# Problem Name
   460→
   461→*Reference:* [Source Title](https://source-url.com)
   462→
   463→Brief description if helpful.
   464→-/
   465→
   466→namespace ProblemName
   467→
   468→/-- Main definition if needed -/
   469→def myDefinition (x : ℕ) : ℕ := x + 1
   470→
   471→/-- The main conjecture -/
   472→@[category research open, AMS 11]
   473→theorem main_conjecture : SomeStatement := by
   474→  sorry
   475→
   476→/-- A variant or special case -/
   477→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   478→theorem main_conjecture.variants.special_case : WeakerStatement := by
   479→  sorry
   480→
   481→end ProblemName
   482→```
   483→

file.content:
# Agent Guidelines for Formal Conjectures

This document provides guidelines for AI agents working on the Formal Conjectures repository.

## Repository Structure

### Main Directories

- **`FormalConjectures/`**: Contains formalisations of conjectures, organized by source:
  - `ErdosProblems/` - Problems from [erdosproblems.com](https://www.erdosproblems.com/)
  - `Paper/` - Problems from research papers
  - `Arxiv/` - Problems from arXiv papers (organized by arXiv ID)
  - `Books/` - Problems from mathematics books
  - `Wikipedia/` - Problems from Wikipedia
  - `OptimizationConstants/` - Problems from [Tao's Optimization Constants](https://teorth.github.io/optimizationproblems/)
  - Other sources as appropriate

- **`FormalConjectures/Util/`**: Repository infrastructure and utilities:
  - `Attributes/` - Defines the `category` and `AMS` attributes
  - `Answer.lean` - Implements the `answer()` elaborator for problems requiring answers
  - `Linters/` - Custom linters for the repository
  - `ProblemImports.lean` - Standard imports for problem files

- **`FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`**: Definitions, lemmas, and basic API suitable for upstreaming to Mathlib:
  - **IMPORTANT**: No `sorry` is allowed in this directory
  - Follows Mathlib's directory structure (e.g., `Topology/`, `Algebra/`, `NumberTheory/`)
  - Contains supporting definitions and lemmas needed for problem statements
  - Code here should be of Mathlib quality and eventually PR-able to Mathlib

## Formalisation Conventions

### Namespaces

Each problem file should define its content in a dedicated namespace:

```lean
namespace Erdos10

-- definitions, theorems, variants here

end Erdos10
```

For variants of a problem, use dotted notation within the same namespace:
```lean
theorem main_conjecture : ... := by
  sorry

theorem main_conjecture.variants.special_case : ... := by
  sorry
```

### The `category` Attribute

Every theorem/lemma should have exactly one `category` attribute indicating its type:

**Values:**
- `@[category high_school]` - High school level math problem
- `@[category undergraduate]` - Undergraduate level math problem
- `@[category graduate]` - Graduate level math problem
- `@[category research open]` - Open research problem (no accepted solution exists)
- `@[category research solved]` - Solved research problem (informal proof widely accepted)
- `@[category research formally solved using <kind> at "link"]` - Formally solved problem
  - `<kind>` must be one of: `formal_conjectures`, `lean4`, or `other_system`
- `@[category test]` - Sanity check or unit test for definitions
- `@[category API]` - Basic theory around a new definition

**Examples:**
```lean
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem riemann_hypothesis : ... := by
  sorry

@[category test, AMS 11]
theorem sanity_check : ¬ SomeBadProperty := by
  sorry

@[category API]
lemma basic_property_of_new_definition : ... := by exact ...
```

### The `AMS` Attribute

Every problem should have at least one AMS subject classification number from the [AMS MSC2020](https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/msc/pdfs/classifications2020.pdf).

**Common subjects:**
- `3` - Mathematical logic and foundations
- `5` - Combinatorics
- `11` - Number theory
- `14` - Algebraic geometry
- `54` - General topology
- `60` - Probability theory

Multiple subjects can be specified:
```lean
@[AMS 5 11]  -- Both combinatorics and number theory
theorem erdos_problem : ... := by
  sorry
```

**In VS Code:** Use `#AMS` command to list all available subjects and their numbers. Hover over a number to see its description.

Full list of AMS subjects
| Code | Name | Keywords |
|------|------|----------|
| 00 | General and overarching topics | general |
| 03 | Mathematical logic and foundations | logic, set theory, model theory |
| 05 | Combinatorics | combinatorics, graph theory, enumeration |
| 06 | Order | order, lattice, poset, partial order |
| 08 | General algebraic systems | universal algebra, algebraic system |
| 11 | Number theory | number theory, prime, arithmetic, diophantine |
| 12 | Field theory and polynomials | field, polynomial, galois |
| 13 | Commutative algebra | commutative, ring, ideal, module |
| 14 | Algebraic geometry | algebraic geometry, variety, scheme |
| 15 | Linear algebra | linear algebra, matrix, vector |
| 16 | Associative rings and algebras | associative, ring, algebra |
| 17 | Nonassociative rings and algebras | lie algebra, jordan |
| 18 | Category theory; homological algebra | category, functor, homological |
| 19 | K-theory | k-theory |
| 20 | Group theory and generalizations | group, finite group, representation |
| 22 | Topological groups + Lie groups | topological group, lie group |
| 26 | Real functions | real analysis, real function, derivative |
| 28 | Measure and integration | measure, integration, lebesgue |
| 30 | Functions of a complex variable | complex analysis, holomorphic, analytic |
| 31 | Potential theory | potential, harmonic function, subharmonic |
| 32 | Complex analysis | several complex variables, complex manifold |
| 33 | Special functions | special function, gamma, zeta, bessel |
| 34 | Ordinary differential equations | ode, differential equation |
| 35 | Partial differential equations | pde, partial differential |
| 37 | Dynamical systems and ergodic theory | dynamical, ergodic, chaos |
| 39 | Difference and functional equations | difference equation, functional equation |
| 40 | Sequences + series + summability | sequence, series, convergence |
| 41 | Approximations and expansions | approximation, expansion, interpolation |
| 42 | Harmonic analysis on Euclidean spaces | harmonic analysis, fourier |
| 43 | Abstract harmonic analysis | abstract harmonic |
| 44 | Integral transforms + operational calculus | integral transform, laplace |
| 45 | Integral equations | integral equation |
| 46 | Functional analysis | functional analysis, banach, hilbert |
| 47 | Operator theory | operator, spectral |
| 49 | Calc. of vars. and opt. ctrl.; optimiz. | calculus of variations, optimal control |
| 51 | Geometry | geometry, euclidean, projective |
| 52 | Convex and discrete geometry | convex, polytope, discrete geometry |
| 53 | Differential geometry | differential geometry, manifold, riemannian |
| 54 | General topology | topology, topological space, compact |
| 55 | Algebraic topology | algebraic topology, homotopy, homology |
| 57 | Manifolds and cell complexes | manifold, cell complex |
| 58 | Global analysis + analysis on manifolds | global analysis |
| 60 | Probability theory | probability, stochastic, random |
| 62 | Statistics | statistics, estimation, hypothesis |
| 65 | Numerical analysis | numerical, approximation, algorithm |
| 68 | Computer science | computer science, complexity, algorithm |
| 70 | Mechanics of particles and systems | mechanics, particle, celestial |
| 74 | Mechanics of deformable solids | elasticity, solid mechanics |
| 76 | Fluid mechanics | fluid, navier-stokes |
| 78 | Optics + electromagnetic theory | optics, electromagnetic |
| 80 | Classical thermodynamics + heat transfer | thermodynamics, heat |
| 81 | Quantum theory | quantum, quantum mechanics |
| 82 | Statistical mechanics | statistical mechanics |
| 83 | Relativity and gravitational theory | relativity, gravity, einstein |
| 85 | Astronomy and astrophysics | astronomy, astrophysics |
| 86 | Geophysics | geophysics |
| 90 | Mathematical programming | optimization, linear programming |
| 91 | Game theory | game theory, economics |
| 92 | Biology and other natural sciences | biology, mathematical biology |
| 93 | Systems theory; control | control theory, systems theory |
| 94 | Information and communication + circuits | information theory, coding theory |
| 97 | Mathematics education | education |

### The `answer()` Elaborator

For problems that ask questions requiring specific answers (not just yes/no), use `answer(sorry)`:

```lean
/-- Does $P$ hold? -/
@[category research open]
theorem problem_name : answer(sorry) ↔ SomeProperty := by
  sorry
```

Note that if the statement of the theorem depends on some variables, then the quantification should
happen *after* the `answer(sorry)`, i.e.
```lean
/-- Does $P(n)$ hold for all $n$? -/
@[category research open]
theorem problem_name : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n, P n := by
  sorry
```
rather than
```lean
/-- Does $P(n)$ hold for all $n$? -/
@[category research open]
theorem problem_name (n) : answer(sorry) ↔ P n := by
  sorry
```


When the problem is solved:
- Replace `answer(sorry)` with `answer(True)` or `answer(False)` as appropriate
- Update the category to `research solved` or `research formally solved ...`

**Note:** Providing a term inside `answer()` does NOT automatically mean the problem is mathematically solved - trivial or tautological answers don't count as solutions.

### File Organization

1. **One problem per file** (with flexibility for closely related variants)
2. **Copyright header required** (see template in README.md, use year 2026)
3. **Module docstring** with references:
   ```lean
   /-!
   # Problem Name

   *Reference:* [Title](URL)

   Brief description if needed.
   -/
   ```
4. **Import structure**:
   - Problem files: Import `FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`
   - ForMathlib files: Import only necessary Mathlib modules
5. If a problem fits in several directories then it should stated in one directory rather than copied
   accross several. In other directories, one can simply add a file with a declaration pointing
   to the original, e.g.
   ```lean
   @[category research open, AMS 11]
   theorem pointer_to_original : type_of% my_original_theorem := by
     sorry
   ```
   See for example `FormalConjectures/GreensOpenProblems/81.lean`.

### Variants and Related Results

Variants should be in the same file as the main conjecture:

```lean
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem main_conjecture : MainStatement := by
  sorry

@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem main_conjecture.variants.weaker_version : WeakerStatement := by
  sorry

@[category test, AMS 11]
theorem main_conjecture.variants.small_cases : MainStatement with (n < 100) := by
  interval_cases n <;> decide
```

## Lean and Mathlib Style Guidelines

### Naming Conventions

Follow [Mathlib's naming conventions](https://leanprover-community.github.io/contribute/naming.html). Unlike Lean 3, Lean 4 uses a combination of `snake_case`, `lowerCamelCase`, and `UpperCamelCase`:

#### Capitalization Rules

1. **Terms of Props** (proofs, theorem names) use `snake_case`:
   ```lean
   theorem fermat_last_theorem : FermatLastTheorem := by sorry
   lemma add_comm (a b : ℕ) : a + b = b + a := by sorry
   ```

2. **Props and Types** (inductive types, structures, classes) use `UpperCamelCase`:
   ```lean
   class HasGδSingletons (X : Type*) [TopologicalSpace X] : Prop
   structure MyStructure where
     field1 : ℕ
     field2 : String
   inductive Color where
     | Red | Green | Blue
   ```

3. **Functions** are named the same way as their return values:
   - A function `A → B → C` is named as though it's a term of type `C`
   - If `C` is a `Prop`, use `snake_case`
   - If `C` is a `Type`, use `lowerCamelCase`

4. **All other terms of Types** use `lowerCamelCase`:
   ```lean
   def leftFactorial (n : ℕ) : ℕ := ∑ i in Finset.range n, i!
   def myFunction (x : ℕ) : ℕ := x + 1
   ```

5. **UpperCamelCase in snake_case contexts**: When something named with `UpperCamelCase` is part of something named with `snake_case`, it is referenced in `lowerCamelCase`:
   ```lean
   theorem fermat_last_theorem : FermatLastTheorem := by sorry
   theorem nat_factorial_pos (n : ℕ) : 0 < n! := by sorry
   ```

6. **Acronyms**: Written as a group in upper-/lowercase depending on the first character:
   - `LE`, `LT`, `GE`, `GT` (when in `UpperCamelCase` context)
   - `le`, `lt`, `ge`, `gt` (when in `lowerCamelCase` context)

7. **Structure fields and constructors**: Follow the same rules (1-6) as top-level declarations

#### Common Naming Patterns

- `add_comm`, `mul_assoc` - operation + property
- `Nat.factorial_pos` - namespace + definition + property
- `foo_of_bar` - derive `foo` from hypothesis `bar`
- `bar_iff_foo` - equivalence between `bar` and `foo`
- `foo_le_bar` - inequality statement

#### Exceptions

Some rare exceptions exist for consistency:
- `Ne` (not `NE`) follows `Eq`
- Intervals: `Set.Icc`, `Set.Iic` (capital `I` despite convention)
- Some legacy structure fields may be lowercase

**Note**: In this repository, most declarations are theorems (terms of Props), so you'll primarily use `snake_case`.

### Code Quality

- **Use Unicode math symbols** where appropriate: `∀`, `∃`, `∈`, `⊆`, `∧`, `∨`, `¬`, etc.
- **Format code properly**: Use consistent indentation (2 spaces)
- **Add docstrings** with the math written in Latex for definitions and main theorems:
  ```lean
  /--
  The left factorial of $n$, defined as $0! + 1! + 2! + ... + (n-1)!$
  -/
  def left_factorial (n : ℕ) := ...
  ```
- **Use `local notation`** for problem-specific notation within namespaces
- **Avoid unnecessary type annotations** when Lean can infer them
- **Use `by` tactic mode** for sorries: `by sorry` (not just `sorry`)
- **Do not try to add a suggested informal proof for an open research problem**
- **Avoid using tactics like `native_decide` in proofs**. These are completely banned in `FormalConjecturesForMathlib`
  and may be considered on a case by case basis for in `category test` statements in problem files
  when the goal is a large computation.

### Imports

- Be specific with imports - don't import more than needed
- In FormalConjecturesForMathlib, import only from Mathlib
- In problem files, import only `FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`, unless you are adding a
   pointer to another problem or need to state an implication.

## Agent-Specific Requirements

### Before Submitting

**CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS:**

1. **`lake build` MUST pass** without errors before submitting for review
   - Run `lake build` in the repository root
   - Fix all compilation errors
   - Ensure all dependencies are properly imported

2. **`sorry` usage restrictions**:
   - ✅ **ALLOWED**: In `FormalConjectures/` for benchmark problem statements
     ```lean
     @[category research open]
     theorem open_conjecture : Statement := by sorry
     ```
   - ❌ **NOT ALLOWED**: In `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/`
     ```lean
     -- WRONG - will be rejected
     def helper_function : Type := sorry
     lemma helper_lemma : P := by sorry
     ```
   - Exception: `answer(sorry)` is allowed as a placeholder for unknown answers

3. **Completeness**:
   - No placeholder definitions (e.g., `def foo : Type := sorry`)
   - No incomplete type annotations or holes
   - All referenced definitions must exist
   - All imports must be correct

4. **Clean code**:
   - Remove commented-out code
   - Remove debug statements
   - No unused imports
   - No unnecessary intermediate definitions

### Quality Checklist

Before considering your work complete, verify:

- [ ] File has correct copyright header (current year)
- [ ] Module docstring with reference links present
- [ ] Namespace properly opened and closed
- [ ] All theorems have `category` attribute
- [ ] All theorems have at least one `AMS` subject
- [ ] Names follow snake_case convention
- [ ] `lake build` succeeds
- [ ] No `sorry` in FormalConjecturesForMathlib/
- [ ] Docstrings present for main definitions
- [ ] Code properly formatted and readable

### Testing Definitions

When adding new definitions to FormalConjecturesForMathlib/, include basic sanity checks:

```lean
-- In FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Topology/MyDefinition.lean
class MyNewClass (X : Type*) : Prop where
  property : SomeProperty X

-- In FormalConjectures/Paper/ProblemUsingMyDefinition.lean
@[category test]
theorem myNewClass_sanity_check :
    MyNewClass SomeConcreteType := by
  constructor
  exact proof_of_property
```

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

❌ **DON'T:**
- Use `sorry` outside of problem statement proofs
- Create files without copyright headers
- Forget to add `category` and `AMS` attributes
- Submit code that doesn't compile
- Add large proofs (this is a benchmark repository, not a proof repository)
- Use camelCase for theorem names
- Create placeholder definitions in FormalConjecturesForMathlib/

✅ **DO:**
- Follow existing file patterns in the repository
- Keep formalisations clean and minimal
- Add references to sources. These should all appear in the module docstring reference list, and
  ideally each conjecture statement docstring should have a reference.
- Use namespaces appropriately
- Test that `lake build` works
- Add variants in the same file as the main problem
- Include basic API for new definitions

## Getting Help

- Check existing files for examples: `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/10.lean`, `FormalConjectures/Paper/Kurepa.lean`
- Use `#AMS` command in VS Code to see available subject classifications
- Refer to [Mathlib naming conventions](https://leanprover-community.github.io/contribute/naming.html)
- See [main README.md](./README.md) for contribution workflow
- See [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md) for CLA requirements

## Example Template

```lean
/-
Copyright YYYY The Formal Conjectures Authors.
    (Replace YYYY with the current year, e.g., 2026 for work done in 2026)

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Problem Name

*Reference:* [Source Title](https://source-url.com)

Brief description if helpful.
-/

namespace ProblemName

/-- Main definition if needed -/
def myDefinition (x : ℕ) : ℕ := x + 1

/-- The main conjecture -/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem main_conjecture : SomeStatement := by
  sorry

/-- A variant or special case -/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem main_conjecture.variants.special_case : WeakerStatement := by
  sorry

end ProblemName
```

content.content:
1→# Formal Conjectures
     2→
     3→[![.github/workflows/push_master.yml](https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/actions/workflows/build-and-docs.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/actions/workflows/build-and-docs.yml)
     4→[![Gitpod Ready-to-Code](https://img.shields.io/badge/Gitpod-ready--to--code-blue?logo=gitpod)](https://gitpod.io/#https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures)
     5→
     6→A collection of formalised statements of conjectures in
     7→[Lean](https://leanprover.github.io/lean4/doc/whatIsLean.html), using
     8→[mathlib](https://github.com/leanprover-community/mathlib4).
     9→
    10→Explore the docs: [Formal Conjectures Documentation](https://google-deepmind.github.io/formal-conjectures/)
    11→
    12→Join our [channel on the leanprover Zulip](https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/524981-Formal-conjectures)
    13→
    14→## Goals
    15→
    16→While there is a growing corpus of formalised theorems including proofs, there
    17→is a lack of open conjectures where only the statement has been formalised. This
    18→would be useful for a few reasons. It could
    19→
    20→*   Become a great benchmark for automated theorem provers and automated
    21→    formalisation tools.
    22→*   Help clarify the precise meaning of conjectures through formalisation.
    23→*   Encourage the expansion of `mathlib` by highlighting needed definitions.
    24→
    25→It is our hope that this initiative will form the seed of a much richer dataset of
    26→formalised conjectures.
    27→
    28→### Note on Formalisation Accuracy
    29→
    30→Formalizing mathematical statements without proofs is inherently challenging.
    31→Subtle inaccuracies can arise where the formal statement might not perfectly
    32→capture the nuances of the original conjecture. To mitigate this issue, we will
    33→rely on careful human review of contributions, and plan to periodically leverage
    34→AlphaProof to help identify potential misformalisations.
    35→
    36→## Contributing
    37→
    38→Contributions are most welcome, consider adding (or even just opening an issue
    39→describing) your favourite conjecture.
    40→
    41→### I'd like to contribute - what can I do?
    42→
    43→There are various ways of contributing to this repository:
    44→
    45→1.  **Adding new problem formalisations**
    46→
    47→    We encourage adding formalisations of open conjectures from all sorts of sources, including:
    48→
    49→    * **Literature:** Textbooks, problem books and research papers (including [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/archive/math)).
    50→    * **Community Resources:** [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unsolved_problems_in_mathematics), [MathOverflow](https://mathoverflow.net/) and the [OEIS](https://oeis.org/).
    51→    * **Problem Lists:** Famous collections ([Millennium](https://www.claymath.org/millennium-problems/), [Smale](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Smale%27s_problems), Yau), [Erdős Problems](https://www.erdosproblems.com/), [Ben Green's list](https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/greenbj/papers/open-problems.pdf), [Kourovka notebook](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1401.0300) or [The Scottish Book](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scottish_Book)
    52→
    53→    We are also interested in the formalised statements of solved variants of
    54→    open conjectures and solved statements from dedicated problem lists.
    55→    While the main goal is to collect conjecture statements, we appreciate the
    56→    inclusion of very short proofs for solved items or counterexamples,
    57→    especially if they are illuminating and testing the definitions.
    58→    Lengthy proofs are outside the scope of this repository.
    59→
    60→2.  **Opening issues with problems that you would like to see formalised.** Such
    61→    an issue should contain links to suitable references, and ideally a precise
    62→    informal statement of the conjecture.
    63→
    64→3.  **Formalise a problem already proposed**. You can find a list of problems ready
    65→   to be worked on in our [list of unassigned new conjectures](https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+no%3Aassignee+label%3A%22new+conjecture%22) or
    66→   [good first issues](https://github.com/google-deepmind/formal-conjectures/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20is%3Aopen%20no%3Aassignee%20label%3A%22good%20first%20issue%22).
    67→   Just pick one and comment on the issue (e.g., "I plan to work on this") to
    68→   have it assigned to you.
    69→
    70→4.  **Improving the referencing and tagging of problems.** For example, adding
    71→    pointers to references in already existing files, or adding additional
    72→    relevant `AMS` subject attributes to statements.
    73→
    74→5.  **Fixing misformalisations.** PRs fixing incorrect formalisations and issues
    75→    flagging problems are encouraged.
    76→
    77→### How to Contribute
    78→
    79→Please see [CONTRIBUTING](./CONTRIBUTING.md) first.
    80→
    81→1.  Open an issue on GitHub specifying what you plan to contribute.
    82→2.  Fork the repository on GitHub.
    83→3.  Add your formalised conjecture(s) in the appropriate file/directory
    84→    structure to a branch in your fork.
    85→    *   Include comments linking to the source of the conjecture (paper,
    86→        website, book).
    87→    *   Use the `category` attribute to specify what category each of the
    88→        statements falls into (see below for more details on this.)
    89→    *   Use the `AMS` attribute to specify what mathematical areas each of the
    90→        statements are related to.
    91→4.  Ensure the code builds (`lake build`).
    92→5.  Submit a Pull Request to the main repository.
    93→
    94→For the use of AI, the [same conventions and precautions as in mathlib](https://github.com/leanprover-community/leanprover-community.github.io/blob/478f0f4b8be577d6d3f913053918551dcf68b1c6/templates/contribute/index.md?plain=1#L68-L70) apply.
    95→
    96→## Usage, Structure & Features
    97→
    98→This is a Lean 4 project managed with `lake` and a dependency `mathlib`. You
    99→first need to
   100→[install elan, lake, lean and if you want vscode](https://leanprover-community.github.io/get_started.html)
   101→and then run
   102→
   103→```bash
   104→lake exe cache get
   105→lake build
   106→```
   107→
   108→### Directory structure
   109→
   110→The directory structure is organised by the type of sources of the conjectures.
   111→There are two special directories:
   112→
   113→-   `FormalConjectures/Util` contains utilities like the
   114→    [`category` attribute](./FormalConjectures/Util/Attributes/Basic.lean), the
   115→    [`answer( )` elaborator](./FormalConjectures/Util/Answer.lean) and some
   116→    linters.
   117→-   `FormalConjecturesForMathlib` contains code potentially suitable to be upstreamed to
   118→    [mathlib](https://github.com/leanprover-community/mathlib4). Here we follow
   119→    mathlib's directory structure.
   120→
   121→### Some features
   122→
   123→#### The `category` attribute
   124→
   125→A tag to mark the category of a problem statement. In this repository, we allow
   126→for the following categories:
   127→
   128→-   Open research problem: a mathematical problem or conjecture for which no
   129→    solution or proof is currently accepted by the mathematical community.
   130→-   Solved research problem: a problem with an established solution.
   131→    This includes problems that have a formal proof within this repository,
   132→    a formal proof of an equivalent statement found elsewhere, or an informal
   133→    solution widely accepted by experts in the field.
   134→-   Formally solved research problem: a research problem with a formal proof.
   135→    Use `@[category research formally solved using <kind> at "link"]` where
   136→    `<kind>` is one of:
   137→    - `formal_conjectures`: solved in this repository (link to commit)
   138→    - `lean4`: solved in Lean 4 (e.g., Mathlib or another repository)
   139→    - `other_system`: solved in another formal system (Coq, Isabelle, etc.)
   140→-   Graduate level problem.
   141→-   Undergraduate level problem.
   142→-   High school level problem.
   143→-   API statement: a statement that constructs basic theory around a new
   144→    definition.
   145→-   Test statement: a statement that serves as a "unit test". These are useful
   146→    to check e.g new definitions or theorem statements.
   147→
   148→This repository targets research level problems. As such, graduate/
   149→undergraduate/high school level problems should only be contributed if they
   150→are directly related to a research level problem (e.g. as a special case,
   151→etc.).
   152→
   153→The tags should be used as follows:
   154→
   155→```lean
   156→@[category research open]
   157→theorem foo : Transcendental ℚ (rexp 1 + π) := by
   158→  sorry
   159→
   160→@[category research solved]
   161→theorem bar : FermatLastTheorem := by
   162→  sorry
   163→
   164→```
   165→
   166→#### The `AMS` attribute
   167→
   168→The `AMS` tag is intended to provide some information about the mathematical
   169→subjects a given statement is related to. For simplicity, we use the main
   170→subjects listed in the
   171→[AMS MSC2020](https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/msc/pdfs/classifications2020.pdf).
   172→
   173→The tag can be used as follows:
   174→
   175→```lean
   176→@[AMS 11] -- `11` means "Number Theory"
   177→theorem flt : FermatLastTheorem := by
   178→  sorry
   179→```
   180→
   181→Within a Lean file, you can use the `#AMS` command to list all the possible
   182→values.
   183→
   184→To determine the subject associated to the tag `AMS foo` in VS Code, you can hover
   185→over `foo`.
   186→
   187→The attribute allows multiple parameters, e.g. `@[AMS foo bar]` is valid.
   188→
   189→#### The `answer( )` elaborator
   190→
   191→Some open questions are formulated in a way that require a user provided answer,
   192→for instance the
   193→[Hadwiger–Nelson problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hadwiger%E2%80%93Nelson_problem)
   194→asks for the minimum number of colours needed to colour the plane such that no two
   195→points exactly one unit distance apart have the same colour. The `answer( )`
   196→elaborator allows us to formulate the problem without deciding for an answer.
   197→
   198→```lean
   199→@[category research open]
   200→theorem HadwigerNelsonProblem :
   201→    UnitDistancePlaneGraph.chromaticNumber = answer(sorry) := by
   202→  sorry
   203→```
   204→
   205→## Problems that require answers
   206→
   207→Note that providing a term inside the `answer( )` elaborator together with a proof that
   208→the statement is true *does not* by itself mean that the problem has been solved. For example, a question
   209→of the form "Which natural numbers satisfy the predicate $P$?" might be formalised as
   210→```lean
   211→theorem myOpenProblem : {n : ℕ | P n} = answer(sorry) := by
   212→  sorry
   213→```
   214→and one can provide trivial answers that aren't mathemetically interesting, e.g. the set
   215→`{n : ℕ | P n}` itself.
   216→
   217→In particular, the question of whether the answer provided corresponds to a mathematically
   218→meaningful solution of the problem is outside of the scope of this repository.
   219→
   220→## Style Guidelines
   221→
   222→1.  Generally speaking, every problem should have its own file, though there is
   223→    some flexibility here (e.g. variants and special cases should go in the same
   224→    file).
   225→2.  Bespoke definitions are allowed, as long as they help clarify problem
   226→    statements. We also encourage contributors to provide some very basic API
   227→    for such definitions as a way to test whether these behave as expected.
   228→3.  Benchmark problems should be stated with the `theorem` or `lemma` keywords.
   229→4.  Every statement should have at least one `AMS` subject tag.
   230→5.  Every file should come with a reference to where the problem was sourced
   231→    from, and be put in the corresponding directory of the repository, e.g. a
   232→    problem sourced from wikipedia should live in `FormalConjectures/Wikipedia`.
   233→6.  When a problem is stated as a question in English, the preferred style is to
   234→    use `answer(sorry)` in the following way:
   235→    ```lean
   236→    /-- English version: "Does P hold ?" -/
   237→    theorem myConjecture : answer(sorry) ↔ P := by
   238→      sorry
   239→    ```.
   240→    This way the informal "Does ...", "Are there ..." or "Is it true that ..." corresponds
   241→    to the `answer(sorry)` in the formalised statement.
   242→
   243→    If the problem has been solved, `answer(sorry)` should be replaced by
   244→    `answer(True)` or `answer(False)` accordingly.
   245→    If the problem is not stated as a question, the following style is preferred:
   246→    ```lean
   247→    /-- English version: "P holds" -/
   248→    theorem myConjecture : P := by
   249→      sorry
   250→    ```
   251→    If the problem has been solved to the negative, then `P` should be replaced with
   252→    `¬ P`.
   253→7.  Every file should start with the following copyright header (replace YYYY with
   254→    the current year):
   255→    ```lean
   256→    /-
   257→    Copyright YYYY The Formal Conjectures Authors.
   258→
   259→    Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
   260→    you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
   261→    You may obtain a copy of the License at
   262→
   263→        https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
   264→
   265→    Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
   266→    distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
   267→    WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
   268→    See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   269→    limitations under the License.
   270→    -/
   271→    ```
   272→    Also consider adding yourself to the list of authors in the AUTHORS file.
   273→
   274→
   275→## Versioning
   276→
   277→This repo will track the monthly tagged releases of mathlib (which correspond to
   278→Lean releases), rather than tracking mathlib master.
   279→
   280→To minimize friction when adding problem statements that need definitions that
   281→are not yet in mathlib, such definitions can be added to the `FormalConjecturesForMathlib`
   282→directory. This ensures that the addition of these problems to
   283→formal-conjectures is not locked to the mathlib release cadence.
   284→
   285→## Licensing
   286→
   287→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors. All software is licensed under the Apache License,
   288→Version 2.0 (Apache 2.0); you may not use this file except in compliance with
   289→the Apache 2.0 license. You may obtain a copy of the Apache 2.0 license at:
   290→https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
   291→
   292→All other materials are licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0
   293→International License (CC-BY). You may obtain a copy of the CC-BY license at:
   294→https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode.
   295→
   296→The content may be based on third party sources and may in some cases include
   297→third party content. The original source for each conjecture is indicated by a
   298→URL within the source file. Third party content may be subject to different
   299→licensing requirements. In particular:
   300→
   301→-   Material from Wikipedia articles, MathOverflow and the OEIS is released under the
   302→    Creative Commons Attribution-Share-Alike License 4.0.
   303→-   Material from bbchallenge.org is used under the Creative Commons Attribution
   304→    4.0 International License.
   305→-   Material from the Equational Theories Project is used under Apache-2.0.
   306→-   Material from arXiv is used under the licence applicable to the relevant
   307→    paper, as indicated at the URL within the source file.
   308→
   309→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, all software and
   310→materials distributed here under the Apache 2.0 or CC-BY license are distributed
   311→on an "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either
   312→express or implied. See the licenses for the specific language governing
   313→permissions and limitations under those licenses.
   314→
   315→This is not an official Google product.
   316→

1→# How to contribute
     2→
     3→We'd love to accept your patches and contributions to this project.
     4→
     5→## Before you begin
     6→
     7→### Sign our Contributor License Agreement
     8→
     9→Contributions to this project must be accompanied by a
    10→[Contributor License Agreement](https://cla.developers.google.com/about) (CLA).
    11→You (or your employer) retain the copyright to your contribution; this simply
    12→gives us permission to use and redistribute your contributions as part of the
    13→project.
    14→
    15→If you or your current employer have already signed the Google CLA (even if it
    16→was for a different project), you probably don't need to do it again.
    17→
    18→Visit <https://cla.developers.google.com/> to see your current agreements or to
    19→sign a new one.
    20→
    21→### Review our community guidelines
    22→
    23→This project follows
    24→[Google's Open Source Community Guidelines](https://opensource.google/conduct/).
    25→
    26→## Contribution process
    27→
    28→### Code reviews
    29→
    30→All submissions, including submissions by project members, require review. We
    31→use GitHub pull requests for this purpose. Consult
    32→[GitHub Help](https://help.github.com/articles/about-pull-requests/) for more
    33→information on using pull requests.

content.content:
1→/-
     2→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.Attributes.AMS
    18→
    19→open Lean Elab Meta Qq
    20→
    21→/-! # Problem Formalisation Attributes
    22→
    23→## The Category Attribute:
    24→
    25→### Overview
    26→Provides information of the type of a statement. This can be:
    27→- A mathematical problem (high school/undergraduate/graduate/research level).
    28→  If this is a research problem then the user is also required to specify
    29→  whether the problem has already been solved.
    30→- An API statement
    31→- A "test" statement
    32→
    33→### Values
    34→The values of this attribute are
    35→- `@[category high_school]` : a high school level math problem.
    36→- `@[category undergraduate]` : an undergraduate level math problem.
    37→- `@[category graduate]` : a graduate level math problem.
    38→- `@[category research open]` : an open reseach level math problem.
    39→- `@[category research solved]` : a solved reseach level math problem.
    40→  The criterion for being solved is that there exists an informal solution
    41→  that is widely accepted by experts in the area. In particular, this
    42→  does *not* require a formal solution to exist.
    43→- @[category research formally solved using <kind> at "link"]` : a formally solved research problem.
    44→   `<kind>` must be one of:
    45→  - `formal_conjectures` : solved in this repository (e.g. filling the `sorry` in the current file).
    46→  - `lean4` : solved in Lean 4 (e.g. Mathlib, or some other repo) as an equivalent statement.
    47→  - `other_system` : solved in another formal system (Roqc, Isabelle, Lean3, HOL, etc.).
    48→- `@[category test]` : a statement that serves as a sanity check (e.g. for a new definition).
    49→- `@[category API]` : a statement that constructs basic theory around a new definition
    50→
    51→### Usage examples
    52→The tag should be used as follows:
    53→```
    54→@[category high_school]
    55→theorem imo_2024_p6
    56→    (IsAquaesulian : (ℚ → ℚ) → Prop)
    57→    (IsAquaesulian_def : ∀ f, IsAquaesulian f ↔
    58→      ∀ x y, f (x + f y) = f x + y ∨ f (f x + y) = x + f y) :
    59→    IsLeast {(c : ℤ) | ∀ f, IsAquaesulian f → {(f r + f (-r)) | (r : ℚ)}.Finite ∧
    60→      {(f r + f (-r)) | (r : ℚ)}.ncard ≤ c} 2 := by
    61→  sorry
    62→
    63→@[category research open]
    64→theorem an_open_problem : Transcendental ℝ (π + rexp 1) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→@[category test]
    68→theorem a_test_to_sanity_check_some_definition : ¬ FermatLastTheoremWith 1 := by
    69→  sorry
    70→```
    71→
    72→## The Problem Subject Attribute
    73→
    74→Provides information about the subject of a mathematical problem, via a
    75→numeral corresponding to the AMS subject classification of the problem.
    76→This can be used as follows:
    77→```
    78→@[AMS 11] -- 11 correponds to Number Theory in the AMS classification
    79→theorem FLT : FermatLastTheorem := by
    80→  sorry
    81→```
    82→
    83→The complete list of subjects can be found here:
    84→https://mathscinet.ams.org/mathscinet/msc/pdfs/classifications2020.pdf
    85→
    86→
    87→In order to access the list from within a Lean file, use the `#AMS` command.
    88→
    89→Note: the current implementation of the attribute includes all the main categories
    90→in the AMS classification for completeness. Some are not relevant to this repository.
    91→
    92→-/
    93→
    94→-- TODO(lezeau): can we/should we do this using
    95→-- `Lean.EnumAttributes` or `Lean.ParametricAttribute` ?
    96→
    97→namespace ProblemAttributes
    98→
    99→/-- The type of formal proof that exists for a problem. This is meant to be used
   100→for the `research formally solved` category. -/
   101→inductive FormalProofKind
   102→  /-- The problem exactly as stated in formal-conjectures has a formal proof.
   103→  The link points to a commit that fills the `sorry` relative to the current
   104→  commit (i.e., the commit where this category is added, or the commit with the
   105→  latest fix for this statement). -/
   106→  | formalConjecturesProof
   107→  /-- The problem is solved in Lean 4 (e.g. in Mathlib or some other
   108→  repository), perhaps as an equivalent statement. -/
   109→  | lean4
   110→  /-- The problem is formally solved in a different system (Roqc, Isabelle, Lean 3, HOL, etc.). -/
   111→  | otherSystem
   112→  deriving Inhabited, BEq, Hashable, ToExpr
   113→
   114→inductive ProblemStatus
   115→  /-- Indicates that a mathematical problem is still open. -/
   116→  | open
   117→  /-- Indicates that a mathematical problem is already solved,
   118→  i.e., there is a published (informal) proof that is widely accepted by experts. -/
   119→  | solved
   120→  /-- Indicates that a mathematical problem is formally solved in this repository,
   121→  with a link to the formal proof. -/
   122→  | formallySolvedAt : FormalProofKind → String → ProblemStatus
   123→  deriving Inhabited, BEq, Hashable, ToExpr
   124→
   125→syntax formalProofKind := &"formal_conjectures" <|> &"lean4" <|> &"other_system"
   126→
   127→def formalProofKind.toName (stx : TSyntax ``formalProofKind) : Option Name :=
   128→  match stx with
   129→  | `(formalProofKind| formal_conjectures) => ``FormalProofKind.formalConjecturesProof
   130→  | `(formalProofKind| lean4) => ``FormalProofKind.lean4
   131→  | `(formalProofKind| other_system) => ``FormalProofKind.otherSystem
   132→  | _ => none
   133→
   134→syntax problemStatus := &"open" <|> &"solved"
   135→  <|> (&"formally" &"solved" &"using" formalProofKind &"at" str)
   136→
   137→/-- Convert from a syntax node to a name. -/
   138→def problemStatus.toName (stx : TSyntax ``problemStatus) : Option Name :=
   139→  match stx with
   140→    | `(problemStatus| open) => ``ProblemStatus.open
   141→    | `(problemStatus| solved) => ``ProblemStatus.solved
   142→    | `(problemStatus| formally solved using $_ at $_) => ``ProblemStatus.formallySolvedAt
   143→    | _ => none
   144→
   145→/-- A type to capture the various types of statements that appear in our Lean files. -/
   146→inductive Category
   147→  /-- A high school level math problem. -/
   148→  | highSchool
   149→  /-- An undegraduate level math problem. -/
   150→  | undergraduate
   151→  /-- A graduate level math problem. -/
   152→  | graduate
   153→  /-- A reseach level math problem. This can be open, or already solved -/
   154→  | research : ProblemStatus → Category
   155→  /-- A test statement that serves as a sanity check (e.g. for a new definition)-/
   156→  | test
   157→  /-- An "API" statement, i.e. a statement that constructs basic theory around a new definition -/
   158→  | API
   159→  deriving Inhabited, BEq, Hashable, ToExpr
   160→
   161→syntax CategorySyntax := &"high_school" <|> &"undergraduate" <|> &"graduate"
   162→    <|> (&"research" problemStatus) <|> &"test" <|> &"API"
   163→
   164→-- TODO(lezeau): do we eventually want to account for the problem's source?
   165→structure CategoryTag where
   166→  /-- The name of the declaration with the given tag. -/
   167→  declName : Name
   168→  /-- The status of the problem. -/
   169→  category : Category
   170→  /-- The (optional) comment that comes with the given declaration. -/
   171→  informal : String
   172→  deriving Inhabited, BEq, Hashable, ToExpr
   173→
   174→/-- Defines the `categoryExt` extension for adding a `HashSet` of `Tag`s
   175→to the environment. -/
   176→initialize categoryExt : SimplePersistentEnvExtension CategoryTag (Std.HashSet CategoryTag) ←
   177→  registerSimplePersistentEnvExtension {
   178→    addImportedFn := fun as => as.foldl Std.HashSet.insertMany {}
   179→    addEntryFn := .insert
   180→  }
   181→
   182→def addCategoryEntry {m : Type → Type} [MonadEnv m]
   183→    (declName : Name) (cat : Category) (comment : String) : m Unit :=
   184→  modifyEnv (categoryExt.addEntry ·
   185→    { declName := declName, category := cat, informal := comment })
   186→
   187→structure SubjectTag where
   188→  /-- The name of the declaration with the given tag. -/
   189→  declName : Name
   190→  /-- The subject(s) of the problem. -/
   191→  subjects : List AMS
   192→  /-- The (optional) comment that comes with the given declaration. -/
   193→  informal : String
   194→  deriving Inhabited, BEq, Hashable, ToExpr
   195→
   196→/-- Defines the `tagExt` extension for adding a `HashSet` of `Tag`s
   197→to the environment. -/
   198→initialize subjectExt : SimplePersistentEnvExtension SubjectTag (Std.HashSet SubjectTag) ←
   199→  registerSimplePersistentEnvExtension {
   200→    addImportedFn := fun as => as.foldl Std.HashSet.insertMany {}
   201→    addEntryFn := .insert
   202→  }
   203→
   204→def addSubjectEntry {m : Type → Type} [MonadEnv m] (name : Name)
   205→    (subjects : List AMS) (informal : String) : m Unit :=
   206→  modifyEnv (subjectExt.addEntry ·
   207→    { declName := name, subjects := subjects, informal := informal })
   208→
   209→/-- Convert from a syntax node to a term of type `Category` and annotate the syntax
   210→with the corresponding name's docstring. -/
   211→def Syntax.toCategory (stx : TSyntax ``CategorySyntax) : CoreM Category := do
   212→  match stx with
   213→  | `(CategorySyntax| high_school) =>
   214→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.highSchool
   215→    return Category.highSchool
   216→  | `(CategorySyntax| undergraduate) =>
   217→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.undergraduate
   218→    return Category.undergraduate
   219→  | `(CategorySyntax| graduate) =>
   220→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.graduate
   221→    return Category.graduate
   222→  | `(CategorySyntax| research $status) =>
   223→    let problemStatus ← match status with
   224→      | `(problemStatus| formally solved using $kind at $link) => do
   225→        Elab.addConstInfo status ``ProblemStatus.formallySolvedAt
   226→        let some n := formalProofKind.toName kind | throwUnsupportedSyntax
   227→        let pfKind ← Lean.Meta.MetaM.run' <|
   228→          unsafe Meta.evalExpr FormalProofKind q(FormalProofKind) (.const n [])
   229→        pure (ProblemStatus.formallySolvedAt pfKind link.getString)
   230→      | _ => do
   231→        let some n := problemStatus.toName status | throwUnsupportedSyntax
   232→        Elab.addConstInfo status n
   233→        Lean.Meta.MetaM.run' <|
   234→          unsafe Meta.evalExpr ProblemStatus q(ProblemStatus) (.const n [])
   235→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.research
   236→    return Category.research problemStatus
   237→  | `(CategorySyntax| test) =>
   238→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.test
   239→    return Category.test
   240→  | `(CategorySyntax| API) =>
   241→    Elab.addConstInfo stx ``Category.API
   242→    return Category.API
   243→  | _ => throwUnsupportedSyntax
   244→
   245→syntax (name := Category_attr) "category" CategorySyntax : attr
   246→
   247→/-- Specifies the type of a statement.
   248→
   249→This is used as follows: `@[category my_cat]` where `my_cat` is one of:
   250→- `high_school` : a high school level math problem.
   251→- `undergraduate` : an undergraduate level math problem.
   252→- `graduate` : a graduate level math problem.
   253→- `research open` : an open reseach level math problem.
   254→- `research solved` : a solved reseach level math problem.
   255→- `research formally solved here "link"` : a formally solved research problem with a permalink.
   256→- `test` : a statement that serves as a sanity check (e.g. for a new definition).
   257→- `API` : a statement that constructs basic theory around a new definition -/
   258→initialize Lean.registerBuiltinAttribute {
   259→  name := `Category_attr
   260→  descr := "Annotation of status of a problem."
   261→  add := fun decl stx _attrKind => do
   262→    let oldDoc := (← findDocString? (← getEnv) decl).getD ""
   263→    let (status, comment) ← match stx with
   264→      | `(attr| category $s) => withRef s do
   265→        let cat ← Syntax.toCategory s
   266→        return (cat, "")
   267→      | _ => throwUnsupportedSyntax
   268→    if status == .research .open then
   269→      let env ← getEnv
   270→      if (env.find? decl).bind (·.value?) |>.any (!·.hasSorry) then
   271→        logWarning "If a problem has a sorry-free proof, it should not be categorised as `open`."
   272→    addCategoryEntry decl status oldDoc
   273→  applicationTime := .afterTypeChecking
   274→}
   275→
   276→syntax subjectList := many(num)
   277→
   278→/-- Converts a syntax node to an array of `AMS` subjects.
   279→
   280→This also annotates the every natural number litteral encountered, with the
   281→description of the corresponding AMS subject (i.e. hovering over the number
   282→in VS Code will show the subject.)
   283→-/
   284→def Syntax.toSubjects (stx : TSyntax ``subjectList) : MetaM (Array AMS) := do
   285→  match stx with
   286→  | `(subjectList|$[$nums] *) =>
   287→    nums.mapM fun (n : TSyntax `num) => do
   288→      let nVal := n.getNat
   289→      let name ← numToAMSName nVal
   290→      Elab.addConstInfo n name
   291→      unsafe Meta.evalExpr AMS q(AMS) (.const name [])
   292→  | _ => throwUnsupportedSyntax
   293→
   294→syntax (name := problemSubject) "AMS" subjectList : attr
   295→
   296→/-- Specifies the subject of a math problem.
   297→
   298→`AMS n` indicates that a problem is related to the subject area
   299→with index `n` in the AMS subject classification.
   300→-/
   301→initialize Lean.registerBuiltinAttribute {
   302→  name := `problemSubject
   303→  descr := "Annotation of the subject of a given problem statement"
   304→  add := fun decl stx _attrKind => do
   305→    let oldDoc := (← findDocString? (← getEnv) decl).getD ""
   306→    let subjects ← match stx with
   307→      | `(attr| AMS $n) => withRef n <|
   308→        Lean.Meta.MetaM.run' (Syntax.toSubjects n)
   309→      | _ => throwUnsupportedSyntax
   310→    addSubjectEntry decl subjects.toList oldDoc
   311→}
   312→
   313→section Helper
   314→
   315→/-- Split an array into preimages of a function.
   316→
   317→`splitByFun f arr` is the hashmap such that the value for
   318→key `b : β` is the array of `a : α` in `arr` that get mapped
   319→to `b` by `f` -/
   320→def splitByFun {α β : Type} (f : α → β) [BEq β] [Hashable β]
   321→    (arr : Array α) : Std.HashMap β (Array α) :=
   322→  Array.foldr addPreimage {} arr
   323→where
   324→  addPreimage (a : α) (m : Std.HashMap β (Array α)) :=
   325→    m.alter (f a) (appendIfExists a)
   326→
   327→  appendIfExists (a) : Option (Array α) → Option (Array α)
   328→  | some arr => arr.push a
   329→  | none => #[a]
   330→
   331→variable {m : Type → Type} [Monad m] [MonadEnv m]
   332→
   333→def getTags : m (Array CategoryTag) := do
   334→  return categoryExt.getState (← MonadEnv.getEnv) |>.toArray
   335→
   336→def getStatementTags : m (Std.HashMap Category (Array CategoryTag)) := do
   337→  return splitByFun CategoryTag.category (← getTags)
   338→
   339→def getCategoryStats : m (Category → Nat) := do
   340→  let cats ← getStatementTags
   341→  return fun c ↦ (cats.map <| fun _ arr ↦ arr.size).getD c 0
   342→
   343→def getSubjectTags : m (Array SubjectTag) := do
   344→  return subjectExt.getState (← MonadEnv.getEnv) |>.toArray
   345→
   346→end Helper
   347→
   348→end ProblemAttributes
   349→
